g.data-narration: Welcome to Module 2: Virtualization of CPU, Memory, and I/O Devices. This is the foundation of cloud computing.
g.text: Module 2: Virtualization of CPU/Memory & I/O Devices

g.data-narration: Virtualization creates virtual versions of hardware, allowing one physical machine to run multiple isolated virtual machines.
g.text: Creating virtual (not actual) versions of hardware components like CPU, Memory, Storage, Network.
A single Host machine runs multiple isolated Guest VMs, each with its own OS.

g.data-narration: The hypervisor or VMM is the software layer that manages hardware resources and isolates VMs from each other.
g.text: Software layer that sits between hardware and VMs. Manages CPU scheduling, memory allocation, and I/O device access.
Ensures isolation between VMs and prevents interference between guest operating systems.

g.data-narration: CPU virtualization allows multiple guest OS to share one physical CPU. Three main approaches exist: Full, Para, and Hardware-assisted.
Full Virtualization uses binary translation to trap and convert privileged instructions. Guest OS remains unmodified but performance overhead exists.
g.text: Hypervisor translates ALL privileged instructions from guest OS into safe instructions for hardware.
Guest OS is unaware of virtualization - no modification needed. Can incur performance overhead.

g.data-narration: Para-virtualization modifies the guest OS to make explicit hypercalls. Better performance but requires OS kernel modifications.
g.text: Guest OS is MODIFIED to be "hypervisor-aware" - makes explicit hypercalls for privileged operations.
Reduces overhead vs full virtualization. Requires modifying guest OS kernel - not all OS support this.

g.data-narration: Modern CPUs have built-in virtualization extensions (Intel VT-x, AMD-V) with root/non-root modes for efficient trapping.
g.text: Modern CPUs include Intel VT-x or AMD-V extensions. Introduces new CPU modes: Root (hypervisor) and Non-root (guests).
Privileged instructions automatically trap to hypervisor - no binary translation or OS modification needed.

g.data-narration: Memory virtualization provides each VM with its own isolated contiguous physical memory space through address translation.
g.text: Each VM needs its own isolated view of contiguous physical memory. Hypervisor maps virtual addresses to real machine addresses.
The guest OS thinks it has direct access to physical memory, but actually accesses pseudo-physical memory managed by the hypervisor.
Guest Virtual → Guest Physical → Host Physical

g.data-narration: Hypervisor maintains shadow page tables that directly map guest virtual addresses to host physical addresses, bypassing guest tables.
g.text: The guest OS maintains its own page table (Virtual → Pseudo-physical). The hypervisor creates a shadow page table that maps directly to machine addresses.
Guest Page Table (managed by Guest OS)

g.data-narration: Hardware support like Intel EPT and AMD NPT accelerates memory translation with nested page tables, reducing software overhead.
g.text: AMD Nested Page Tables (NPT) and Intel Extended Page Tables (EPT) hardware-accelerate the two-level translation.
CPU hardware handles guest virtual → guest physical → host physical translation directly, dramatically reducing overhead.
All in hardware - no software overhead!

g.data-narration: I/O device virtualization comes in three types: Full emulation, Para-virtualized, and Direct passthrough. Each balances compatibility and performance.
Full emulation creates a virtual generic device (like Intel E1000 NIC). Guest uses standard drivers but overhead is high due to context switching.
g.text: Hypervisor emulates a well-known generic hardware device (e.g., Intel E1000 NIC). Guest OS uses standard driver.
High compatibility but inefficient - many context switches between guest and hypervisor for I/O operations.

g.data-narration: Para-virtualized I/O uses Virtio framework with frontend/backend drivers for direct hypervisor communication, achieving near-native performance.
g.text: Virtio framework provides abstract simplified device interface. Guest uses front-end drivers, host uses back-end drivers.
Efficient communication between guest and hypervisor - near-native performance. Standard for Linux/KVM.

g.data-narration: SR-IOV allows physical devices to present multiple virtual functions, assigned directly to VMs for near-native performance in high-throughput tasks.
g.text: Single Root I/O Virtualization (SR-IOV) - Physical device presents multiple "virtual functions" (VFs).
Each VF assigned directly to a VM - bypasses hypervisor entirely. Near-native performance for network/GPUs.

g.data-narration: Here's a comparison: Hardware-assisted CPU, NPT/EPT for memory, and Virtio or SR-IOV for I/O provide the best modern performance.
In summary, virtualization decouples software from hardware, enabling resource pooling, on-demand access, and multi-tenancy - the hallmarks of cloud computing.
g.text: • Virtualization enables cloud computing by abstracting hardware resources
• Modern systems use hardware-assisted virtualization for best performance
Virtualization decouples software from hardware, enabling the cloud!
